data.title: HOOY: Attractive Entry Before Robinhood's Q4 Earnings (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

When it comes to single-stock option ETFs, I believe that timing is very important. These are high-risk instruments that can be disastrous if held through unfavorable price momentum. So when I previously covered the YieldMax HOOD Option Income Strategy ETF (HOOY), I issued a hold rating since the fund traded near all-time highs at the time. Since my last coverage, HOOY's share price has declined by more than 34% and now sits near its all-time lows. Therefore, I wanted to revisit the ETF's current appeal now that the momentum has shifted.

Looking at the performance since inception, we can see that HOOY's share price has now eroded by about 17.7%. As Robinhood Markets (HOOD) continues to see its share price decline, HOOY's momentum has followed. However, the inclusion of all distributions takes the total return up to 59.4% over the same time frame. The main appeal of HOOY at this time is the high starting dividend yield of 54.5%. The fund also issues payouts on a weekly basis, which can be ideal for high risk income investors. However, there are some clear tradeoffs that investors should consider before initiating a position just because of the high yield.

Now that HOOY has fallen from its highs, we can see how HOOY's price erosion has really increased over recent months. This is a common threat to many of the different YieldMax funds, which makes it a difficult fund to buy and hold through all market conditions. However, I believe that HOOD has some potential catalysts to help it regain some of its positive momentum. As a result, HOOY should be able to partially participate in this price upside and the distributions will rise as the underlying option strategy produces larger premiums. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that HOOY implements to generate the amplified income needed to support distributions.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, HOOY now has net assets of $146M and an expense ratio of 0.99% of assets. As the share price has fallen, we can see how the underlying assets under management has also declined from its highs. Since distributions remain so high, they may be actively accelerating the pace at which the assets are trending downward, which can be a serious threat to the fund if the downward momentum continues for a prolonged period of time.

HOOY implements an option writing strategy against HOOD and collects option premiums, which are then used to fund the high distributions. This fund's option writing strategy also allows HOOY to replicate the daily price change of HOOD over time. However, a synthetic approach comes with its own set of vulnerabilities that must be considered. The most notable risk is that a synthetic option writing strategy means that HOOY doesn't actually own any common shares of HOOD.

Since options are being written against an asset that the fund doesn't actually own, this increases price instability and means HOOY isn't structurally capable of seeing a meaningful level of price growth over time. When assessing the holdings within HOOY, we can see that the fund is most heavily made up of cash and U.S. Treasuries to maintain its liquidity. There are no common shares of HOOD within the fund, but there are active options:

  • HOOD US - 02/20/26 C$135
  • HOOD US - 01/30/26 C$117

As we can see, the active options have different expiration dates. Based on the holdings, HOOY will typically write options with an expiration that is ~30 days out. HOOY will typically implement an OTM (out-of-the-money) strategy, which means that the selected strike price will be slightly above the current market price of HOOD. This allows HOOY to partially participate in some upside price growth through bullish movements, but the price is still ultimately capped at whatever the selected strike price is.

So if HOOD currently trades around ~$104 per share, HOOY's option will only capture the price growth up to $117 per share. In a scenario where HOOD's price jumped sharply to $125 per share, HOOY would essentially miss out on all of the growth above the strike price of $117 per share. HOOY is essentially trading most of its upside growth in exchange for income that can be distributed out to shareholders.

Performance Insights & Outlook

Since the upside is capped, investors should anticipate that nearly all of their returns in HOOY will come from the fund's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions. However, HOOY's high dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to high total returns over time. If you are bullish on the outlook of HOOD, you would probably be better off simply owning the common shares of HOOD. As we can see below, HOOD's total return of 94.6% significantly outperforms HOOY's total return of 59.4% over the same time frame.

If the fund's share price continues to decline, this will eat into an investor's total return over time. Unfortunately, HOOY's structure means that a declining share price is almost a guarantee over a longer holding period. While the fund has capped upside growth, there is no structure in place to protect against downward markets. So this means that HOOY will feel the full impact of declines, while having trouble to recover on the upside movements that follow. Over time, this means that the fund's share price will typically trend downward. When you add in the high rate of distributions that are paid, which directly impact the share price, it is almost a certainty that HOOY will continue to trend downward.

However, this structure is by design and this is why I want to put emphasis on the fact that timing of entry is extremely important. I believe that the absolute best time to accumulate shares in HOOY is when the stock is trading flat and Robinhood may have a catalyst that takes it higher in the near-term. I believe that the outlook for HOOY can essentially be summarized by these three possible outcomes:

  1. Robinhood Markets Rises = HOOY will also partially participate in the growth. Depending on how strong the positive price momentum is, there's the chance that HOOY's distributions will also grow larger. However, HOOY is still likely to underperform HOOD over time due to the capped nature of its options.
  2. Robinhood Trades Flat = HOOY's share price may stay within a consistent trading range and when including distributions, HOOY has the potential to outperform. However, this relies on HOOD resisting any rapid downturns. Depending on how large the distributions are, NAV erosion may still occur.
  3. Robinhood Declines = HOOY is likely to experience capital erosion over time. The distributions may help offset these losses, but the share price is highly unlikely to recover unless there's a strong rise in HOOD. This is the phase we are currently in and we've already seen how the share price can rapidly erode over time.

HOOD is set to report its Q4 2025 earnings in mid-February and I believe there is a good chance that the report will be solid. HOOD's normalized EPS estimate comes in at $0.69 per share, with 12 positive revisions in the last 90 days. For reference, Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $148.53 per share, which indicates a potential upside of more than 42% from its current level. When HOOD reports its earnings, I believe there are some potential catalysts that the business has in its favor.

One of the most notable things to me would be the expansion of prediction markets. Robinhood has an easy way for users to interact with prediction markets and this will continue to expand as HOOD allows users to participate in almost anything, ranging from the outlook of the economy, politics, sports, and more. We also have the fact that HOOD finalized its acquisition of Bitstamp in mid-2025, which can help the business capitalize on growing interest within the crypto markets. While this may take a few quarters to materialize into higher earnings, it still serves as a forward looking catalyst that can be the next leg of growth. I think that any optimism in these operating channels can reverse HOOD's current price direction.

If I am correct, HOOY will be able to partially participate in HOOD's share price growth. As the price trends upward, HOOY will also be able to capture larger option premiums, assuming that volatility rises over the next quarter. While HOOY is still likely to underperform HOOD, this provides income investors with the opportunity to strategically capture a high rate of income and positive total returns.

Dividend Risks

The latest declared weekly distribution is $0.4542 per share, bringing the estimated annualized yield to ~54.5%. Since new distributions are being declared every week, the annualized yield may be different by the time you read this article. Looking at the dividend history below, we can see how the payouts have consistently shifted over time based on the underlying momentum of HOOD. So when HOOD was trading near all-time highs, HOOY was able to pay out much larger distributions since the option strategy was able to capture much larger premiums from the upswings. Conversely, the payouts have become smaller as the share price has declined. Please note that HOOY switched from monthly distributions to weekly distributions in late October.

Lower payouts are the primary risk for income investors over a longer holding period. If the underlying NAV declines, HOOY will have fewer assets that it can use to generate income with its options. Therefore, the distributions will be reduced over time to reflect the lower income generation. Before HOOY shifted to a weekly payout schedule, the fund previously issued a massive distribution of $6.90 per share back in July of 2025. Since the fund has fallen from its highs, the distributions have significantly declined.

Current $0.4542 per share payout X 4 weeks = $1.8168 payout

YieldMax sends out a distribution breakdown email every week and this is the clearest way to keep up with how much of the distribution is made up of return of capital. As we can see below, this payout was made up of 95.11% return of capital. While return of capital distributions are ideal because they are not directly taxed as income, they also directly reduce the fund's share price by an equivalent amount. Therefore, these distributions can actually cause the fund's share price to fall more rapidly than HOOD.

For example, HOOD traded at its all-time high on October 9, 2025. Since then, the share price has maintained a consistent downward trend. As we can see below, HOOY's share price has actually declined a lot quicker because of the return of capital distributions being paid. While HOOY's total return slightly outpaces HOOD, an investor's capital would still erode much quicker with HOOY.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on HOOY to a buy now that the share price has fallen from its highs. With HOOD's upcoming earnings, I believe that a positive report can spark optimism around the outlook of the business and help reverse momentum. Since HOOY's price growth is capped over a longer holding period, I believe that investors must strategically choose to time their entry and look for opportunities where the underlying stock may increase in value. While I have no expectation that HOOY will outperform HOOD over a longer holding period, I believe that a strategic position in HOOY can help us capture a high level of income and take advantage of volatility in the short-term.

data.title: ASML: Reality Check After Doubling To All-Time Highs (Downgrade)
data.description:
From A Contrarian “Buy” To A Stretched Valuation

Almost seven months ago, stocks of European chip-machine manufacturer ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) traded at half of today’s price. The stock had sold off on a lack of visibility into 2026 amid comments on unclear timing of customer capex. Nevertheless, management’s long-term vision until 2030 remained intact, prompting me to view this as nothing more than short-term uncertainty or conservative management of expectations, rather than a structural headwind. With the stock having traded around fair value at 626 euros, I took a contrarian stance, considering a “Buy” worthwhile.

Since then, ASML stock has almost doubled to 1,233 euros, now already pricing in successful years ahead and thus warranting a downgrade to “Hold” for the European chip-machine leader dominating the market for patterning equipment in wafer production.

Record Bookings Meet Improved Visibility

ASML released Q4-25 and FY-25 numbers this week with two especially positive readings.

First, 2026 is now officially expected to be a year of growth, with a mid-point revenue guidance of €37 billion ($43 billion at current rates), implying double-digit YoY growth. Even the more cautious guidance implies mid-single-digit growth, while the upper-end target range would even come with a growth expectation of 20%. Gross margin is expected to come in at 52%, roughly in line with the latest reports, and the 2030 outlook with a mid-point 10% top-line CAGR and gross margins expanding to a mid-point of 58% remains untouched.

Second, ASML’s book-to-bill ratio reached its highest value in at least seven quarters during Q4-25. Net bookings surged to an all-time high of €13.2 billion during the quarter against similarly record-breaking net system sales of €7.6 billion. This marks a notable reversal from book-to-bill ratios below 100% in prior quarters.

Although the number of lithography systems sold decreased YoY, the implied average selling price increased sharply, underscoring the company’s pricing power and leading standing in the industry. Service sales remained flattish during Q4, but with regards to an ever-increasing installed base, I expect this to be a transitory plateau.

Coming back to bookings, the massive order intake leads to a backlog of now €38.8 billion, significantly above 2025 revenues of €32.7 billion, supporting the newly ignited growth story. According to management, “many of our customers have shared a notably more positive assessment of the medium-term market situation” amid “more robust expectations of the sustainability of AI-related demand.”

But Valuation Is Now Ahead Of Its Time

And with the growth story intact, immediate shareholder returns appear almost negligible at this point. Dividend yield is 0.5%, and while an approved buyback program of up to €12 billion over the next three years at first sounds a lot in absolute terms, it would only reflect 1% of today’s market cap per year. Both lead us to valuation.

Based on a standardized approach (operating cash flow minus capex according to Seeking Alpha), TTM free cash flow of $13 billion increased 20% versus half a year ago. Although this is notably above my intentionally conservative estimates, at the same time, the share price and enterprise value almost doubled, naturally leading to significantly increased valuation.

An enterprise value of $551 billion now corresponds to 42x TTM free cash flow, translating to a 2.4% free cash flow yield (down from 3% last time). At the same time, my rounded WACC estimate for ASML remains elevated at 12% (even up from 11% last time) against the backdrop of increased beta and geopolitical instability, which ASML might be exposed to. As a result, the required growth residual is now approaching 10%, which appears highly ambitious.

Viewed differently, I argue that ASML’s share price is now roughly three years ahead of itself, whereas in July 2025, it was only reasonably ahead of itself by about one year.

As of July 2025, ASML’s 20-year average P/E and P/C were 26. These historical multiples would have been reached in roughly a year’s time on a forward basis.

As of today, ASML’s 20-year average P/E and P/C are similar at 27 and 26. Yet at today’s valuations (50x and 38x) and based on consensus estimates, it would require forward multiples three years out to see historical values again.

Takeaway

ASML is fully on track fundamentally. It became evident that blurry guidance from mid-2025 for 2026 was cautious but not detrimental to the company’s 2030 goals. As this short-term uncertainty was priced out again, ASML surged to all-time highs. At this point, however, the share price has reached valuation levels that historically marked somewhat of a mid-term peak.

As a result, I am downgrading this leading chip-machine manufacturer business to a “Hold” on the back of stretched valuation. Furthermore, the company is exposed to international political tensions, while at the same time, it might be an interesting non-U.S. play for investors looking for a company with low USD exposure. U.S. investors would also profit from FX effects on the stock if one wants to assume a continued weak USD against the Euro. I remain bullish about the company yet turn cautious on the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Target: Best Thing To Do Now Is Do Nothing At All
data.description:
It was a gamble when I upgraded Target Corporation (TGT) to a buy rating and took a position in it in my previous coverage. Three months later, my view was not proven to be baseless considering its 12% returns in a relatively short period. But I advise you not to let your guard down, because TGT still lacks growth drivers to sustain further increases.

Now that it’s already above $100 again, it is essential that I revisit this stock and reassess my position here, whether or not to buy more or lighten them. Valuation is still reasonable, but it’s already getting closer to my previous TP or target price. Meanwhile, technicals are still bullish despite the recent downtrend.

Technicals: Overbuying and Profit-Taking, Not a Bearish Reversal

The short answer is no, if you ask me. Why? TGT is still generally increasing with higher highs and lows. The current price is still above the first key support of $93.99. So, the upward momentum still holds as of this writing. In addition, the stock price is still above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which suggests that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. It is also worth noting that the 50-day SMA is now increasing and has already crossed above the 200-day SMA.

This can tell us two things. First, recent market sentiments have remained generally optimistic. Second, bullish views in the past three months are stronger than in the past year. This can tell us that buyers saw an opportunity to enter during Q4 2025.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI can tell us more about the downtrend. The MACD line is trending downwards below the signal line. This means that selling volume is increasing, while buying volume is decreasing. The histogram is within negative territory, which implies that selling volume has outpaced buying volume recently.

Lastly, RSI is very interesting. This suggests that the current downtrend is not necessarily a reversal but the effect of the cautious stance of the market.

When the price reached its high of $111, the stock also became overbought. This opened a chance for profit-takers to take advantage of the situation. Until now, profit-taking is still evident and may continue until it becomes close to overselling. After all, the price is still above $100, and some investors may feel that it’s still high for their risk appetite.

I’d also like to highlight that its trend is still consistent with the price, so we cannot detect an early bearish divergence.

Valuation

As I looked into its valuation, things became more interesting. After focusing on the Dividend Discount Model, I proved that technicals are still in sync with valuation. If you remember, my target price (TP) in my previous coverage was $110.95 ($99.41 with a 10% risk consideration). When the stock price hit $111, the downtrend immediately began, which could tell us their strong correlation.

With that, I updated my Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is still $4.56 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $1.14. Meanwhile, my estimated dividend growth rate is 7.88% based on the 2021-2025 CAGR. I also updated its cost of equity.

Risk premium is still 7.0% to be conservative and consistent with my previous coverage. But note that the risk-free rate is now 4.3% compared to 4.1% before. This is in line with the current 10-year treasury yield.

Lastly, my estimated Beta is now 1.08, which implies that TGT has become less volatile recently. Using all these numbers, my new TP is $114.57. This is higher than my previous TP. However, their gap is still narrow. Somehow, this justifies the market reaction and profit-taking when the price reached $110.

I also updated its price ratios and focused on P/E and P/S. Using P/E, the stock trades at 12.33x its EPS versus the five-year average of 18.87x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $155.66. We cannot simply say that it’s because of 2022 since its P/E appears to be the outlier in the data set. If we exclude it, the average will be 15.99x, which will reduce the TP to $131.92. Either way, TGT will still be cheaper. Also, its P/E today is the cheapest since 2020. The same applies to P/S Ratio, which gives a TP of $174.26.

However, this may not automatically lead to an uptrend. After all, the ratios only compare the cheapness or expensiveness of TGT relative to previous years. Also, it does not reveal its intrinsic value. The thing is, the market typically cares more about the direction of the EPS and sales. As you can see, the EPS and sales per share TTM are lower than in 2023 and 2024. So it’s not surprising that the stock price trend follows the trend. If we base it here, the assumption is that the EPS and sales for FY 2025 should increase, so the stock price will increase.

If we base it on my financial projection for February 2026, TGT may still take some time to rebound. Considering its performance last Q3, I believe that my financial projection in my previous coverage is still valid and conservative. Based on the improving YoY revenue trend in the past two quarters, I set my revenue decrease at -1%. With that, my estimated revenue high is $105.52B. For my revenue low, I simply set a revenue decrease of -5% to derive $101.24B. Meanwhile, I estimated COGS (72%) and operating expenses (23%) as a percentage of revenue. Lastly, I estimated depreciation and capex using the straight-line method. With that, my NOPAT and EBITDA are still lower than in February 2025. Hence, the stock price may stay within that range.

Technicals and Valuation, So What?

At this price, I say that it’s already a bit too late and also a bit too early to sell. As an investor here, it’s tricky to decide whether to buy or sell TGT. But sometimes the best thing to do is not do anything at all. If you’re my reader who followed my advice to buy at $89-91, I don’t advise you to add to your position. Lightening your shares can be logical, but I also won’t advise you to give it up completely. The downside risk is still tolerable if you ask me. Also, we cannot yet conclude that the recent price dip signals a reversal as I explained earlier. Profit-takers are still on the move at this point. Again, the uptrend still holds, and the price remains above the key support level. If it breaks below it, you may allot about 5-10% below $93.99 as your stop-loss price. So, that will range from $84.58 to $89.29.

Another consideration is its ample dividends. At $4.56, TGT boasts a yield of 4.38%. In addition, the payout ratio remains decent at 59.68%. This means that only 60% of its earnings are used to pay dividends. The remaining 40% are part of retained earnings. This can be used to repay maturing borrowings, repurchase shares, and acquire fixed assets. The thing is, dividends are still sustainable, so I’d like to take advantage of it for now.

Risks

TGT still relies heavily on its physical stores, so its business model is still mainly brick and mortar. This makes it sensitive to inflation and cost pressures, which can affect its pricing flexibility. Today, inflation is 2.7%, which is still close to the 14-month high of 3.0%. This means that prices are still increasing, so purchasing power and operating costs may still be affected.

Competition is still a big challenge. It must watch out for Walmart (WMT) as another brick-and-mortar store with lower and sometimes predatory pricing. This can take away a portion of TGT’s demand. Also, Macy’s (M) and Nordstrom may become its direct competitors. We know that TGT sets its prices below these two premium retailers but higher than WMT and many dollar shops. However, inflation may distort pricing differentiation, especially if sales prices can no longer withstand cost pressures.

How About Amazon? It’s Not What You Think

I know that many investors are very wary of Amazon (AMZN). Even I watch out for its predatory tendencies. AMZN may squeeze TGT in convenience moat and logistics. Ad dollars may also be a factor if brands spend more on AMZN ads than retail promotions from TGT. This can affect its margin leverage.

However, the idea of Amazon killing Target is not simple and correct. There are pressures and overlapping factors, but this is not an existential knockout. After all, TGT is still defensively strong in terms of physical stores as an advantage. AMZN mainly exists in the online space, although it has some physical stores. With thousands of physical stores, TGT capitalizes on same-day pickup, easy returns, and drive-up. This is an aspect that AMZN cannot completely replicate yet. So, I think it’s WMT that TGT should watch out for.

Risk Mitigants and Core Strengths

Aside from its defensive plays against AMZN, I believe TGT can still hold its ground against WMT. Target has its own private labels, like Cat & Jack and Good & Gather. This can help TGT ensure brand loyalty, which can also be its edge over WMT. If you ask me, these brands still feel exclusive for Target. This is something that WMT cannot fully crack. Also, TGT still works and wins on apparel, home decor, and aesthetics. Impulsive buying, or what we often say, “I didn’t plan to buy this, but I did,” works more for TGT. After all, most of its products target affluent and brand-sensitive customers.

Considering all this, it’s not surprising that TGT maintains a decent performance despite inflationary headwinds. Its net sales amounted to $25.27B, down by 1.5% YoY from $25.66B. But this was better than in H1 2025, with a six-month YoY revenue decrease of 1.9%. Its services remained the core strength, especially advertising. F&B and hardlines also helped partially offset the weakness in other merchandise. This shows that its essential goods still have a strong demand.

In addition, TGT remains efficient considering the decrease in COGS. This is quite impressive amid stubborn inflation and an increased number of stores. In fact, its percentage of the combined amount of COGS and SG&A decreased to 76.6% versus 77.1%. This means that TGT was able to reduce its cost sensitivity. I think this can be rooted in two factors. First is the increased portion of digital sales versus in-store sales. Second, TGT is increasing its concentration on mid-sized stores rather than superstores. While this was enough to offset the decrease in sales, it still helped TGT to remain profitable as net income remained high at $689M.

Another core strength of TGT is its liquidity. Its operating cash flow of $1.127B last Q3 was enough to cover capex, borrowing repayments, and share repurchases. TGT complements it with prudent borrowing management to ensure it has sufficient cash. Currently, its cash level remains high at $3.8B. This is still more than enough to cover the maturing borrowings within a year.

In addition, its debt maturity profile remains decent. Note over 90% of the total amount will not mature within a year. Its issuance of $1.5B this year has a maturity of at least 10 years. The remaining $500M has a maturity of two years. Its maturity profile is still in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 1.5x, which means that TGT only needs 1.5x years to repay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. If we only use 70% of EBITDA, which is $5.65B ($8.07B x 70%), the ratio will still be decent at 2.25x. This proves that TGT still has enough capital buffer to sustain its increasing operating capacity and dividends.

Lastly, TGT may benefit from the recent policy easing cycle. Lower interest rates can reduce the repricing of TGT’s variable-rate borrowings, which can improve its liquidity. Also, this can make credit cards more affordable and accessible, which can support discretionary spending. Moreover, it can boost economic activity, which can also increase employment and wages to bolster consumption.

Conclusion

Target Corporation is not a perfect investment considering the macroeconomic risks and intense competition it experiences. But its operational efficiencies, robust fundamentals, strategic pricing, and strong branding power allow it to get through these challenges. Its valuation is not exactly cheap but still reasonable with sustainable dividends. Technicals show some weakness but do not indicate a bearish reversal yet. All things considered, TGT is a hold for now.

data.title: Dollar Index Hits 4-Year Low And Traditional Wisdom Can Mislead This Time
data.description:
A macroeconomic development I have been monitoring lately is the weakening of the dollar index (the DXY index as shown in the next chart). As seen, DXY has been hovering around a 4-year low (of roughly 96 as of this writing). Traditional investment wisdom posits that a weaker U.S. dollar serves as a tailwind for equity markets. The wisdom certainly has its merits and also finds a good amount of support from historical data (and I will recap the gist in a minute). But the key of successful investing lies in the understanding of the applicability range of any wisdom as much as its limitations.

Against this backdrop, my goals in this analysis are mainly twofold. First, I want to identify the top reasons that can make this wisdom stop working under current conditions. Second, I also want to explore some ideas for investors to hedge the further weakening this time.

Traditional wisdoms about weaker dollar

Among the many reasonings for the traditional wisdom, a key one is rooted in the mechanics of multinational corporate earnings and global competitiveness. When the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) falls, the reasoning is that theoretically it can/should trigger a beneficial translation effect for S&P 500 companies. This reason is especially relevant today as many of them generate a large portion of their revenues overseas. As an example, the next chart shows the share of S&P 500 revenues from outside the US annually from 2002 to 2024 (the data is provided by Apollo Academy). During this 22-year period, the portion fluctuated but the overall trend is upward. As of 2024, about 41% of S&P 500 company revenue is generated abroad, compared to about 35% in 2002. The portion is likely to increase further as more products become digital services. A second key reason behind this wisdom is that a weaker dollar could also make American exports more price-competitive abroad.

Now, the counterarguments. To refrain from rambling, I will limit myself to the top two counterarguments on my list. The first one is this: even if the traditional wisdom works in the long run, it has no predictive power in the short run at all in my experience. Near term DXY and equity movements – say in a timeframe of 1 or 2 years - are largely driven by geopolitical and economic events. As an example, the next chart shows the correlation between DXY and SPY month total return in the past 3 decades or so. There is no obvious bias in either direction that I can discern and the long-term average is close to 0.

This time could be different fundamentally

My second reason is more fundamental and structural. The traditional wisdom, again even if it works, is meant for an orderly depreciation of the dollar. The current weakening of the DXY in my view is more instability driven. The cause of the weakening makes a fundamental difference in my view.

Unlike past DXY cycles, the current slide is more likely due to investors’ concerns – especially foreign investors - over a systematic monetary dollar debasement and a potential loss of the dollar's reserve currency status. As a reflection of my assessment, the first chart below (provided by the Federal Reserve) shows a secular decline if U.S. dollar’s weight as foreign reserves (and the rise of other currencies in the meantime). As seen, the latest reading is around 58 percent, compared to a peak level of around 72 percent in 2001. The second example is provided by the IMF (International Monetary Foundation) in the second chart below. As seen, the decline is even more dramatic when the weight is adjusted for exchange rates and interest rates.

When the weakening is caused by weakened confidence about and demand for the dollar itself, it is net negative in my experiences (from studying similar cases of the countries who suffered loss of confidence and reserves status in their currencies) for both US equity and bonds. In this case, the weakening dollar reflects a breakdown of the U.S. exceptionalism narrative in my mind. It could trigger capital outflows and valuation reassessment for US equity. The downside risks are further compounded by the heightened valuation risks for the major equity indices in the US (including the DJI, SP500, NASDAQ, and even small cap (RTY). Flagship market funds such as SPY and QQQ have started suffering sizable outflows lately (about -$4.1B for QQQ and about -$10.9 for SPY as shown in next two charts). Overall, the US equity market has also been experiencing net outflow according to Reuters data below.

Reuters News (Jan 23): U.S. equity funds shrank by $5.26 billion as outflows partly reversed an inflow of $28.17 billion, received in the previous week. Investors, meanwhile, purchased European and Asian equity funds of $10.22 billion and $3.89 billion, respectively in the most recent week.

Other risks and final thoughts

When the weakening of DXY is due to confidence in the dollar, it could also cause pressure on US bond pricing too. As I detailed in a recent article, I strongly urge investors to consider the scenario of much higher treasury bond yields in 2026. In particular, I see 10-year Treasury rates reaching 6% as a plausible scenario in 2026. Among the various reasons behind this forecast, the continued loss of the dollar’s reserves status is a key factor.

If you share my above thoughts, let’s explore a few actionable hedging ideas. Exposure to gold is an obvious one on my list. Unlike the correlation (or the lack of) between DXY and SPY in the near-term, the historical relationship between DXY and GLD has an obvious skewness both in the long- and also short-term as shown in the next chart. As seen, these two assets move in opposite directions most of the time, with a negative correlation coefficient that has historically averaged between -0.42. The correlation remained close to -0.90 for extended period of time. As long as gold is priced in dollars (I don’t see this changing in the foreseeable future), I expect such a strong negative correlation to remain. As a second idea, investors could also consider the use of put options, either on equity funds such as SPY or bond funds such as TLT. Currently, the implied volatility (IV) in both funds is unusually low by historical standards in the options market. The IV underestimates the true risks in my view given my above analysis and the many geopolitical conflicts ongoing, creating an asymmetric return/risk profile for option buyers.

To conclude, this analysis examines the traditional wisdom on the relationship between a weaker dollar and equity returns. My goal is to A) argue why the traditional wisdom can stop working under current conditions, and B) explore some actionable ideas for investors to hedge this scenario. In a nutshell, the gist of my argument is that the cause of the weakening matters as much as – or even more so – than the weakening itself. I see this round of weakening as being caused by a set of fundamentally different forces than in DXY’s previous cycles. As such, I urge investors to not apply the wisdom in their investment decisions this round.

data.title: Unicycive: 'Buy' Rating On NDA Resubmission OLC And Potential Best-In-Class Profile
data.description:
I'm initiating Unicycive Therapeutics (UNCY) with a "Buy" rating, and this is based on its ability to have its resubmission of its New Drug Application [NDA] for oxylanthanum carbonate [OLC] accepted by the FDA for review. This drug is a phosphate binder for the treatment of hyperphosphatemia in patients with chronic kidney disease [CKD] on dialysis. The reason why I believe it deserves this rating is because it is going to be a quicker pathway to approval, if granted this time around. Why is that? That's because the FDA has granted OLC as a Class II complete response, which only stands with a 6-month review time. With that being said, the FDA had set up a Prescription Drug User Fee Act [PDUFA] date of June 29, 2026.

My bullishness stems from the fact that in the prior submission that was rejected, there was no detail of the clinical studies being an issue. Moreover, a Complete Response Letter [CRL] was given to OLC on the basis of a manufacturing issue with a plant inspection. The third party manufacturing plant was set to regain compliance with the FDA, and this is what allowed the NDA resubmission to happen. The clinical data was not cited as part of the problem that the company had faced. Another item to note is that it has another drug in its pipeline with huge potential as well.

This would be UNI-494 as a novel nicotinamide ester derivative and a selective ATP-sensitive mitochondrial potassium channel activator. The novel mechanism of action [MOA] is that it restores mitochondrial function, which might help with the unmet medical need of acute kidney injury [AKI]. What's so special about this program? Well, the main item of importance to note is that if the company is somehow able to obtain marketing approval for UNI-494, it would be the first and only approved therapy on the market for these AKI patients.

CRL Given But Steps Taken To Rectify Manufacturing Issue

The major problem for this company stemmed from the fact that it received a complete response letter [CRL] for its phosphate binder oxylanthanum carbonate [OLC] for the treatment of hyperphosphatemia in patients with Chronic Kidney Disease [CKD] on dialysis. As I noted above, even though a CRL was given, I believe there is a good chance that it will receive marketing approval of this drug next go around. There are several reasons why this would be the case.

The first reason is because the company immediately took the step to set up a Type A meeting with the FDA to align on potential next steps needed to get to the point of being able to resubmit its NDA of OLC for these particular CKD patients.

The second reason is because the CRL only cited an issue with the company's third-party manufacturer. This was said to have been an issue when there was a cGMP inspection of this third-party manufacturing vendor, and deficiencies were found in the process. The point here is that the company ran multiple studies to get to the point of being able to file, and none were flagged for any issues. Such studies that were completed as part of the FDA's 505[b][2] pathway were as follows:

  • Phase 1 healthy volunteer study
  • Bioequivalence study for healthy volunteers
  • Tolerability study of OLC in CKD patients on dialysis
  • Preclinical studies
  • Chemistry, manufacturing, and controls

Speaking of the FDA's 505[b][2] pathway, this is an important item to note. This pathway allows a company to rely partly on previously achieved clinical studies for a drug, and utilize some studies of its own to gain a quicker pathway towards regulatory approval. For example, if OLC is granted marketing approval then it would share the same product label as reference-listed drug [RLD] Fosrenol [lanthanum carbonate]. This pathway is afforded to a similar drug that might for example offer some advantages over an existing one [Unicycive Therapeutics Corporate Presentation January 2026, slide #6]. In this case OLC has smaller tablets and can be swallowed whole as differences.

The third and final reason is because it was just announced today, January 29, 2026, that the FDA had just accepted the NDA submission of OLC for the treatment of hyperphosphatemia for patients with chronic kidney disease [CKD] on dialysis. Not only was this NDA accepted, but it was immediately considered to be a Class II complete response, which only carries a 6-month review period. With that being said, the FDA has set the PDUFA review date to be June 29, 2026. The FDA has up until then or before then to decide if OLC should be granted marketing approval for these specific CKD patients.

Despite Available Therapies There Is Still An Unmet Medical Need

Chronic Kidney Disease [CKD] is characterized as the kidneys losing the ability to filter out blood, leading to a buildup of waste in the body. Early symptoms are not major and can be fatigue or swelling of the body. Two possible reasons why someone might end up with CKD are as follows:

  • High blood pressure
  • Having diabetes

CKD gets really bad when a person reaches a state known as end-stage kidney failure. At this point, a person only has two available options which are a kidney transplant or dialysis. With dialysis, a person is required to have a machine filter out blood several times per week. The problem is that even with dialysis being present, this alone can still not remove enough phosphorous from the blood. When there is an excess amount of phosphorous in the body it leads to hyperphosphatemia. This is a bad thing because it can lead to brittle bones for starters. Even worse, a buildup of phosphorous on lungs, heart, and blood vessels could lead to a heart attack or stroke.

What has to work with dialysis are drugs known as phosphate binders. Even though there are 6 FDA-approved phosphate binders, they fail to help patients achieve target serum phosphorous levels [Unicycive Therapeutics Corporate Presentation January 2026, slide #9]. It makes sense, because patient compliance isn't there or non-existent at all. The average [median] amount of pills necessary for these CKD patients per day is 19. This brings me to the final reason why I think this company deserves a "Buy" rating. It is the ability to have a potential best-in-class phosphate binder with several competitive advantages. Several major advantages of OLC are a lower number of pills necessary, the ability to swallow pills instead of needing to chew them, and smaller-sized pills.

Financials

Unicycive Therapeutics is definitely good in terms of cash. That's because along with the announcement by the FDA of the acceptance of its OLC drug for the treatment of hyperphosphatemia of CKD patients with dialysis, it laid out its cash position. It stated, with respect to its unaudited financials position, it ended 2025 with $41.3 million in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments. It believes that it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into 2027. The company only burns roughly $7.4 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $3 million in R&D expenses and then $4.4 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Unicycive Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the NDA resubmission of OLC as a phosphate binder to treat hyperphosphatemia in CKD patients on dialysis. Even though the CRL of this drug was given solely on the stated basis of a third-party manufacturing issue upon a cGMP inspection by the FDA, there is no assurance that the FDA will end up granting marketing approval of it for the treatment of these specific CKD patients.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the commercialization of OLC, if granted U.S. marketing approval by the FDA. The risk here is that even with all of the competitive advantages I have listed up above, such as lower pill burden, smaller pills, and easier to swallow types, there is no guarantee that these will lead to substantial revenues being generated for it. Nor that OLC still won't face stiff competition from the already 6 FDA-approved phosphate binders.

The third and final risk to consider would be the other drug from its pipeline, which is UNI-494. This drug is being developed to treat patients with Acute kidney Injury [AKI]. It is believed that this drug could restore mitochondrial function and be beneficial in helping to treat patients with AKI. The risk here is that there is no assurance that this MOA will play out well in subsequent human studies. Thus far, a phase 1 study was done and showed the drug to be safe and tolerable. Plus, the drug was shown to have rapid conversion to nicorandil. The importance of this is that it shows its potential as a fast-acting therapy and one that could prevent delayed graft function [DGF]. There is no guarantee that these effects will be observed in the next set of studies.

Conclusion

Unicycive Therapeutics is being initiated with a "Buy" rating. I think that there is a good chance that it will receive FDA approval of OLC for the treatment of hyperphosphatemia in CKD patients on dialysis. This goes back to what I noted above in that the CRL only cited an issue with the third-party manufacturer. I want to preface this by saying, though, that this doesn't guarantee U.S. marketing approval of this drug with certainty. It is not out of the realm of possibilities for the FDA to find a new issue it didn't cite the first time around. While the chance of this happening is small, it is still a major risk nonetheless that should be considered. If this company were to receive another CRL, there is no telling what would happen with this program of OLC targeting this specific group of CKD patients. With the resubmission of the NDA of OLC for the treatment of hyperphosphatemia in CKD patients on dialysis, plus the established PDUFA review date of June 29, 2026, I believe that this company's stock deserves a "Buy" rating.

data.title: Metro: Repeats Last Year's Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Santa already nudged us into upgrading Tesco (TSCDY) after the post-holiday slump. Could Metro Inc. (MRU:CA) be setting up a second late-holiday surprise?

That’s exactly what crossed my mind when I looked at the results and what came after.

The stock was down around 5% on the 27th after the company released earnings at the bell. Revenue beat by about $6.8 million, but EPS missed by $0.04, which was enough to spook the market.

The real difference between Tesco’s slump and what we’re seeing with Metro shows up in profitability.

And that's the catch. Metro’s pressure ties back to the same Toronto distribution center issue I flagged in my last write-up.

Sales still nudged up at a low single-digit pace, but a good chunk of the margin literally melted away after a refrigeration system at one of its Toronto distribution centers unexpectedly stopped working.

To put some color on it, they pulled off a 9.1% EBITDA margin with EBITDA basically flat year-over-year. If you strip out the Toronto issue, margins would’ve been closer to 9.5%, which would’ve topped last year’s 9.3%.

That’s why, from where I sit, this doesn’t feel structural at all.

One thing that hasn’t changed is that hard discounters like Loblaw’s (L:CA) No Frills keep taking small bites out of Metro’s market share. Metro’s response has been Super C in Quebec and Food Basics in Ontario.

The big question here is whether yesterday’s drop earns this Dividend Aristocrat an upgrade from Hold to Buy.

My last target was $101–$105 and the stock nearly got there.

Back at around $91, the upside is back in double digits, and dividends plus buybacks push total return 20%. This feels a lot like the setup I laid out in my article last October.

Forget the Macro, Focus on the Micro

Look, I've been saying for a while that discretionary spending in Canada is holding up better than you'd expect, even with consumer confidence in the gutter and the labor market starting to soften.

You can see it in the data.

Restaurants and apparel are still putting up positive same-store sales and guiding well. Groceries, though, are a bit trickier.

Inflation, in this case, is raising the temperature of competition, because it’s hitting right where consumers feel it most—their daily food bill.

Headline inflation came in at 2.4% in December, but food inflation was running at 6.2% as higher costs kept getting pushed down the chain.

This pressure likely isn’t going away anytime soon.

Across multiple earnings calls, management teams have been flagging that many contracts are set to be renewed later this year, after retailers temporarily held back some price increases to protect Christmas traffic.

And this is where the story really shifts to the micro.

Take one of Canadians’ favorites (and one of mine too), coffee, for example.

By the end of 2025, even good old coffee had gotten almost 30% more expensive year-over-year, as droughts in Brazil and Vietnam squeezed supply and tariffs piled on top.

Since coffee is a recurring purchase, this creates a big opening for private labels like Loblaw’s No Name or Metro’s Selection.

Still, it’s not hard to see why consumers feel squeezed (a year ago we could find the same brands $2 or more cheaper than today).

But do you know what’s interesting?

QSRs like Tim Hortons, under RBI (QSR), just pushed through their first symbolic coffee price hike in three years—a tiny $0.03 bump, basically low single-digit inflation.

The funny thing is that over the past few years we’ve watched a chunk of QSR traffic get swallowed by grocery stores.

Now, with food inflation biting, the traffic is slowly returning.

Restaurants are starting to win back share by giving up a bit of margin on lower-ticket items and leaning harder on cross-selling and upselling (something grocery retailers simply can’t do very well when most of their products are not considered Essentials).

Another interesting point to consider is beef.

I mentioned in my Ultimate Guide to 2026 that this is a real headache for restaurants.

Here, I want to give you a perspective from the retailers' point of view.

Just like in restaurants, beef is a big item in the shopping cart (people see it as a high value-added protein).

But when almost every cut is experiencing double-digit inflation, it's not hard to see shoppers switching from time to time, especially in lower-income households, and opting for cheaper proteins.

In this scenario, the average check per customer plummets in three ways:

  1. Shoppers rotate away from beef into chicken, pork, and other cheaper proteins.
  2. Larger cuts of beef are replaced with smaller packs.
  3. Higher incidence of promotional prices or bundles.

Under normal circumstances, when inflation is more evenly spread across the basket, Metro would probably lose fewer transactions while still benefiting from the nominal lift in same-store sales.

That’s not what we’re seeing this time. The shopping basket is shrinking, not increasing!

All of this goes a long way in explaining why discounters keep crushing conventional banners, as I told you in the last article.

Was the Slump Deserved?

As I mentioned earlier, on the top line, Metro didn’t really disappoint Wall Street in the first quarter of FY 2026.

Sales were $5.3 billion, up 3.3% year-over-year. That’s a touch slower than last quarter, but not very different from what we’ve been seeing throughout FY 2025.

We can start breaking this down by looking at same-store sales.

Food purchases grew 1.6% in the quarter and 1.9% if you adjust for Christmas. They didn't give us much color beyond that, but the CEO said that transactions decreased and were offset by the average check (probably more by inflation than by the mix).

Comparatively, Loblaw and Sobeys (EMLAF) have not yet released their year-end results, but will likely continue to have a gap with Metro in food.

Pharmacy is still being carried by GLP-1 and other prescription drugs, which kept same-store sales growing at a healthy 3.9% year-over-year.

The following chart will show this better:

Now let’s get to what really matters here, the margins. I have a strong hunch this is what actually spooked the stock after earnings.

As I mentioned earlier, EBITDA barely moved this quarter, up just 0.2% to $482.6 million. The main drag was the Toronto incident, which created about $21.6 million in pre-tax headwinds (management had guided to roughly $30.6 million last quarter, so the hit ended up being less painful than expected).

Even with all that noise, the 9.1% EBITDA margin they pulled out of the hat still sits well above the five-year average of 8.7%.

Keep in mind that even though the EBITDA margin came in a bit lighter than expected, it was only about 20 bp below the nine-quarter average of 9.3%.

But did Metro continue to generate healthy cash flow even with this compression of margins?

After all, the whole point of owning a defensive grocer is that it should keep generating FCF.

This quarter they posted $117.8 million in OCF, or $369.1 million if you strip out changes in working capital.

Both numbers came in lower than last year, which makes sense given the margin pressure we just talked about, but the 5.9% decline in adjusted OCF is probably the cleanest way to look at what’s really happening operationally.

Even after Capex dropped from $76.5 million to $50.3 million, FCF still compressed from $89.4 million to $67.5 million.

Now, this wasn't enough to cover the $234.4 million they returned to shareholders, with $79.2 million in dividends and $155.2 million in buybacks, so they had to tap $166.1 million in new debt and still ended the quarter with a cash shortfall.

Here is an overview of it:

Even though this isn't great, you have to look at it in context.

  1. Seasonally, the first quarter usually puts pressure on FCF because of working capital, which almost always leads to a cash deficit, as we've seen in the past two years as well.
  2. The $3.1 billion debt represents a Debt/EBITDA ratio of approximately 1.6x based on the EBITDA of the last twelve months. At least for now, the increase in debt doesn't really look like a problem for Metro.
  3. If you consider the adjusted FCF (excluding operating assets) of $318.8 million, they would be able to cover the $234.4 million without needing to issue debt.

So, aside from the margin compression, I don’t really see any cash generation issues at Metro.

Post-Slump Opportunity?

Being very careful here, little has changed since the last article.

I know the slump is scary, but let's be very objective here.

To begin with, I had projected revenue of $22.74 billion for FY 2026. Looking at the current Wall Street consensus, that has edged slightly lower to $22.73 billion. Along the same lines, I had projected margins in the 9 to 9.5% range, and based on what they pulled out of the hat this quarter, that still looks very much in line.

Being very precise, we would have to trim the EBITDA projection from a range of $2.04 to $2.16 billion to $2.04 to $2.15 billion. In other words, almost nothing has changed here either.

Assuming this holds, I see no reason to change my FCF projection of $1.45 to $1.5 billion, which implies a return to mid-to-high single-digit growth.

What we need to tweak slightly is the EPS. The projection is that it will now be at $5.13, implying a CAGR of ~4% until FY 2034.

If we then plug in a 12x EBITDA multiple, assume no deleveraging, and keep 217 million shares outstanding, we get to a price target between $98.50 and $105. Basically the same setup as before.

Now think about it. The upside is 8 to 15%. Add in mid-to-high single-digit dividends and share buybacks, and you’re looking at roughly a 20% return at the midpoint.

Appetizing enough to change the rating back to Buy.

Be Aware of the Risks

To me, the real risk isn't a one-off problem like the Toronto DC. It’s whether margins start to compress for structural reasons.

A question worth asking is whether pushing harder into discount with banners like Super C and Food Basics could end up eating into margins, especially as they go head-to-head with No Frills.

I see them offsetting this in the short to medium term through private labels, efficiency gains, and top-line growth. Over the long run, however, margins could drift back toward the historical 8.5% to 9% range, which would put the stock roughly at fair value today, or maybe slightly undervalued.

I think a lot of this pessimism is already baked into the stock, along with the pressure from lower basket prices and commodity inflation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cameco Corporation: Stretched Valuation, But High-Quality Business
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Cameco Corporation has been on a strong uptrend the past year. Both because of a very successful earlier acquisition and also because of the rising spot price of uranium. However, buying a $54 billion company, which is up 168% on a 12-month period, I think opens up risk. I’ll admit that the growth in the next few years should be very strong, thanks to one of the main investments CCJ has, but also from rolling over older long-term contracts in its uranium segment to newer ones, locking in a higher average sales price. The fact remains that buying at 46x 2029 earnings is risky unless you have an earlier position and can stomach the downturn and still be green. But because of the unique operating comparison here, capturing the entire value chain, it’s hard to argue a sell case too. Which is why I’m going with a hold here as I initiate coverage on CCJ.

Operations - Global Footprint, Capturing the Entire Value Chain

CCJ is not just a mining company, driving revenues and earnings from having the price of uranium rise. It’s become a lot more than this now. I’ve covered quite a few energy companies before, and most of these were in the oil and gas industry, so I figured I could make the comparison here with CCJ. So think of CCJ as a vertically integrated company that has both the upstream, midstream, and downstream as part of its operations. It controls the entire value chain from exploration to refinement and making the plants.

The supply chain starts in northern Saskatchewan, where it mines the uranium. Here it is then also milled and turned into what the industry often calls “Yellowcake.”

Canada has the world's largest deposits of high-grade uranium with grades of up to 20% uranium, which is 100 times greater than the world average.

Cigar Lake, which is the name of the mine in Canada, has the highest grade of uranium in the world. Even though Canada does not have the largest reserves of uranium in the world, it wins out by having the largest concentration of high-quality ones. This raises the sales prices per kilo and overall makes the Cigar Lake far, far more valuable. I should say that the figures as well from the table are from 2019, which seems to be the latest collective figure for reserves by country.

As for the production rate, these figures are from 2022 and show how Kazakhstan is by far the largest producer and miner of uranium in the world. The country began mining the mineral back in 1943 but surged in 2006 when the annual production went from below 5,000 tonnes to over 20,000 in a matter of 10 years. It seems to have remained stable here as well since then. I found it a bit interesting as well that Kazakhstan is going to deplete their reserves at a much faster pace than any other country. I don't think you should assume any company you invest in will be around in 50 years; it’s too long of a timespan.

But, having said that, it seems unreasonable to assume that some form of CCJ won’t be around when they have access to such a large reserve of uranium. The point I’m trying to make here is that Canada or other countries, for that matter, will have to fill the void where Kazakhstan used to be.

The rise in production came at the same time the spot price and long-term price of the mineral surged to record levels. Prices we still haven’t seen since then, even though many minerals are making decade highs or new all-time highs altogether, like gold and silver.

There are essentially two ways uranium is priced. Spot prices are based on that delivery will be done within 1 year; long-term is generally more than 2 years out, leading to a drag sometimes on the prices, just as the chart shows above.

A Changing Market Where the Westinghouse Acquisition Adds Substantial Exposure

With Kazakhstan on track to finish their existing reserves in less than 50 years unless they can find more, there will be a new country that sits at the top of the global uranium production. Canada and CCJ have the opportunity here to do that.

Demand-related developments continue to suggest growing support for the nuclear industry, with 31 countries pledging to triple their nuclear power capacity by 2050.

With that we also have the fact that the nuclear capacity would be tripling by 2050, less than 30 years away. The current count of nuclear reactors in the world is 439, according to a presentation from CCJ. If we go back to the vertical integration of CCJ, we can look at the downstream, which is the part that concerns this the most. In 2023, it finalized, along with Brookfield Asset Management (BAM), the acquisition of Westinghouse Electric. This is no ordinary company. It makes the technology and provides the services for making nuclear reactors. This is perhaps where the growth case will be if the global reactor count should triple in the next 30 years or so.

In the Q3 report from 2025, CCJ cites that it expects Westinghouse to generate $525 - $580 million adjusted EBITDA. Because CCJ owns a 49% stake and the remaining is being held by Brookfield, there is a division here. The cited figure seems to be for the full year, that being the part CCJ receives.

In reality, though, Westinghouse is still relatively shaky from quarter to quarter in terms of earnings, which has to do with customer project completion and certain milestones being met. On a quarterly basis, CCJ’s share meant a $32 million net loss, but on a 9M basis it was instead a $32 million net positive.

we use adjusted EBITDA as a performance measure for Westinghouse. In the third quarter of 2025, our share of Westinghouse’s adjusted EBITDA was $124 million, compared to $122 million in the third quarter of 2024

On a quarterly and adjusted basis, it looks a lot better. In the energy sector, the median EV/EBITDA multiple sits at 6.81x right now, on a forward basis. This implies that the enterprise value that CCJ owns is worth $3.762 billion (using the midrange of the 2025 guidance). In reality, it’s valued at a lot more considering the consolidated EV of CCJ is $54 billion right now.

If we look at the other 2 segments, which are “Uranium” (upstream) and “Fuel Service” (midstream), they together reported $244 million in Adjusted EBITDA, or $976 million per year. Allocating the same multiple as before, the EV would be $6.646 billion. This would make it about 2x more valuable than the investments held in Westinghouse.

The point I am trying to make here is that if we use the same ratios in terms of valuation, meaning 63% for the upstream and midstream and 37% for the investment stake, we can gauge what the market currently thinks each part is valued at. More or less, at least. At an EV of $54.99 billion, it implies that 49% in Westinghouse is worth $20.34 billion, or 36x EBITDA. The connection I want to make is to the previous growth of the company and whether this high of a premium actually makes sense right now, even when considering where the nuclear sector is heading.

As mentioned earlier, on a pure YoY basis measuring Q3, the growth was minimal in terms of adjusted EBITDA, rising only 1.6%. On a 9M basis, however, the growth was much more impressive, rising 77%. If that keeps up, then yes, the current valuation looks a lot more reasonable, I think. Considering also that these types of companies often have incredible revenue visibility thanks to the backlog they hold.

Based on the estimates for future reactors, around 850-900, and an average cost of $1 billion, the sector will be one of the largest in the world. The cost per reactor can vary between $50 million to $3 billion, so I chose to use a figure on the lower end there.

One of the main arguments as to why Westinghouse could in fact be a powerhouse here is the deal it has made with the U.S. government valued at over $80 billion, announced last year in October. It’s the consequence of the rapid rise in AI data centers and the prospect that these will need huge amounts of energy to be able to keep up and for regular citizens not to have to pay 2x or 3x more per month in electricity bills. What some also forget is that for each of these reactors that Westinghouse builds, they also sign long-term agreements for both fuel and service. This locks them in and ensures revenues will continue for a decade to go since most reactor lifespans are between 30-40 years or more.

The Type of Reactors We Use Are Going to Be Vastly Different

When most people think about nuclear reactors, they see a massive cylinder that looks to be the size of a mountain; at least that’s where my head normally goes. The fact is that this is changing quite drastically. In 2027, Westinghouse will be making their AP300 reactor commercially available, which is a small modular reactor with a 300 MW capacity, about one-third the capacity of their standard AP1000 reactors.

Nuclear is the cheapest type of electricity and will likely stay that way for a long time as well. I think wind and solar will come very close, but even if so, the consistent energy output of nuclear power makes it the superior choice. But what makes this particularly important now is the rising energy demand in the US. Bloomberg predicts that data centers will grow from consuming 34.7 GW in 2024 to 106 GW in 2035. To put that into perspective, the US alone uses about 438 GW per year.

Don’t Bet on Spot Prices Rising Forever

Before looking at the valuation for the company, I think it’s worth going over the upstream and midstream parts of CCJ. After all, it’s a legacy business in a sense.

Looking at the table, we can see how CCJ was aggressively hoarding between 2018 and 2021, building up its inventory levels. I’ve chosen to use the spot price to measure the value of the inventory. CCJ doesn't disclose their method, but I think this serves the purpose of showing what occurred in the past.

By looking at the production volumes compared to the sales price, we can see a mismatch, even when acknowledging that the inventory levels skyrocket. In essence, CCJ acted more like a trading house than a mining company then. The nearly 20 million lb difference between production and sales in 2020 shows this exact thing playing out. It has since then normalized, and CCJ is at decade-low levels in terms of inventories because of the rising price of uranium and the company's ability to take advantage of this by selling.

Looking at the realized price and revenues, we can see that after 2021, CCJ seems to have begun reducing the inventory stake, which would have been made up of mostly cheap uranium, bought at $30/lb or so, and now could be sold at much higher, in 2025 100% higher even if this is the case.

What this means is that CCJ is returning to being a lean miner, where I think inventory levels will continue to fall and help ensure the company generates higher revenues, but because of the rapid rise in spot prices, the strategy that has now worked well is not possible to repeat the same way again. You’d have to bet that the spot price will double.

According to the World Nuclear Association‘s latest biennial Nuclear Fuel Report, uranium demand for nuclear reactors is projected to surge by 28% by 2030, climbing from approximately 67,000 metric tons in 2024 to nearly 87,000 tons annually.

Uranium production has increased drastically globally, jumping 28% YoY. This is just miners around the world wanting to get in on the action, and this normally results in the price falling drastically. The bulls might argue that because the demand for nuclear over the long term is so great, this won’t be the case. On the contrary, the reserves, as we’ve seen, are strong, and there are several countries with undeveloped mines and reserves still. Meaning, production can afford to rise further without necessarily endangering the global reserves that much. This argues for the spot price falling in the near to medium term instead.

Valuation

I think it’s very hard to argue that CCJ is anything other than a hold right now. It has been on an impressive run the past 12 months, but right now the valuation feels stretched. I showed earlier how the company, just based on EV/EBITDA, looks very rich as well, mostly because of the growth with Westinghouse. But that can’t carry the entire company, I think. Even though an $80 billion partnership will help a lot, it’s going to be over several years, and rather than help make the current valuation more appealing, all I think it really does is ensure the company has an even stronger backlog.

If that is worth paying 46x 2029 earnings, I’m hesitant to say either yes or no. On one hand, the company is growing rapidly. On the other hand, it would take a long time to actually realize that upside because the current valuation is so stretched. The price would have to stay still for 3-4 years, during which, as an investor, I’d get no return. A hold makes more sense, and I say this knowing that there might be a period of little returns, but suggesting that a rapidly growing company like CCJ with plenty of long-term tailwinds is a sell is now going too far. Suggesting a buy instead opens up too much risk.

The Full Picture

I’m pretty sure this is the first nuclear stock I cover on Seeking Alpha, and I don't think there would have been a better one than Cameco. The stock has been on fire as deals with Westinghouse have come out, and the spot price of uranium continues to climb.

As I’ve laid out, though, the current valuation is a stretch, and rating this a hold I think is the most fair decision here. Its unique operating composition makes it a long-term high-conviction stock, but as I said, the valuation drags it down to a hold.

data.title: Valmont Industries: An Interesting Derivative Of An AI/Utility Boom
data.description:
Shares of Valmont Industries (VMI) have been on a tear, as they have become a derivative of the AI/utility boom as well. This is, in the sense that its utility part of the infrastructure business provides essential transmission and distribution & substation products.

That more than warrants an update on a dated thesis, which goes back to the summer of 2023, when I concluded that continued strength was displayed.

Ever since, topline momentum has been soft, but real margin gains are delivered upon, as accelerating momentum in the utility business creates an interested setup, although quite a bit of good news has been priced in already.

Serving Infrastructure & Agriculture

Valmont is a producer of various infrastructure & agriculture solutions. The Nebraska-based business employs some 11,000 workers who generate over $4 billion in sales from no less than 80 manufacturing locations.

End markets are largely related to infrastructure, making up about three quarters of sales, complemented by a billion agriculture segment. The business is largely focused on North America, where some 70% of sales are generated, complemented by smaller exposure to the rest of the world.

The core $3 billion infrastructure segment is nearly half comprised of utility solutions, such as the aforementioned transmission, distribution, and substation products. Nearly a third of this segment comes from L&T products, involving lighting and traffic signs for roads. Coatings, telecom, and solar are smaller operations within the segment. The remaining billion-dollar agriculture market is 90% made up of irrigation equipment and parts, complemented by technology and services, this being a much more global business.

Over the past decade, the company has grown sales by more than 50% from $2.6 billion to $4.0 billion, all while high single-digit operating margins have risen to low double-digit margins. Meanwhile, about 10%-15% of the shares have been bought back, creating a triple driver behind earnings per share growth. However, a big part of this topline growth was a post-pandemic inflation wave, as peak revenues of $4.3 billion in 2022 have been stagnant in the years following.

2024 - A Base

In February of last year, Valmont reported its 2024 results, a year in which revenues were down just over 2% to $4.08 billion, with declines concentrated in the agriculture segment. That looks worse than it is, amidst some disinflation in some input costs and strong execution, with adjusted operating margins up no less than 160 basis points to 12.9% of sales and earnings per share up 15% to $17.19 per share. This number is clean, coinciding with the reported GAAP numbers.

Net debt of $568 million was completely manageable, trailing EBITDA by a wide margin, with that number reported at $648 million. The company guided for 2025 sales between $4.0-$4.2 billion, including a 150 basis point headwind from a strong dollar (at the time). This was driven by stronger organic growth in the infrastructure segment, with earnings seen at a midpoint of $18 per share in 2025, plus or minus eighty cents.

Shares jumped to the $370 mark after these numbers were released, as shares traded at 20 times anticipated earnings, in part as the company has effectively been delivering sequential margin gains for years now.

2025 - A Roller Coaster

Shares soon fell to the $250 mark amidst the tariff talks and concerns on the impact on the business, after shares have steadily risen to current levels of $445 per share, all happening in a rather steady fashion.

The company started the year on a softer note with a 0.5% fall in constant currency terms in the first quarter. While second-quarter adjusted sales were not much better, the company hiked the midpoint of the full-year earnings guidance to $18.50 per share.

Third quarter reported sales were reported up 2.5% to $1.05 billion, aided by a softer dollar, as well as driven by a growth in the utility segment surpassing 10%. This is also seen in the backlog, which rose by 20% since the start of the year to $1.7 billion, largely related to the utility space as well.

While the company maintained the full-year sales outlook, the earnings guidance was hiked further to a midpoint of $19.10 per share. Net debt has come down to $504 million, with trailing EBITDA reported at $636 million.

With shares having risen to current levels, it is fair to say that while the margins have been strong since 2025 and improved performance of the utility segment is very evident, those expectations have risen substantially as well. Shares now trade at a 23 times earnings multiple, as the question is how fast sales and earnings growth will accelerate in 2026, as investors are anxiously awaiting comments on that, likely made when the 2025 results are released in February.

What Now?

As of the third quarter, the utility business represents roughly 37% of the total company, as its double-digit sales are badly needed, with sales of the remaining activities being flat at best. This makes that growth, which is now reported just north of 10%, need to continue to meet higher expectations.

There are some signs of that, with the backlog up $293 million in dollar terms year-to-date. If we can attribute $200 million to the utility business, that suggests that roughly $1.5 billion in utility business is seeing real momentum here, with backlog increasing more quickly than reported sales growth, indicating that growth might accelerate in 2026.

This creates a roadmap for sales to advance by mid-single digits in 2026, assuming that the weak agriculture markets stabilize, and while I expect outsized earnings growth, that is very much needed. If the company can deliver on 10%-20% earnings growth, that could result in earnings between $21-$23 per share, for a 20 times forward multiple. That is reasonable, but real achievements have to be made in order for earnings to grow to such levels.

That looks quite reasonable, but expectations have moved up on this stock as well, although a 20 times forward earnings multiple with modest leverage looks very reasonable. However, one should realize that this is just 40% an AI/utility player at this point. This makes me truly interested in keeping a closer eye on the business, hoping for dips, to play the AI market this way instead of directly with the high-fliers.

data.title: Blackstone: Trump's Home Buying Ban Is Noise (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In late November, I reiterated my Hold rating on Blackstone Inc. (BX) and you can see below that the stock has actually just slipped below the price when my update was published. In that article, I found robust fundamentals but I believed the valuation to be excessive. Some readers have correctly pointed out in the comments section that Blackstone is asset-light and so a higher P/B ratio versus other mangers may not necessarily mean overvaluation. I'll take that into consideration in today's analysis.

Below, it is shown that Q4 was generally a solid quarter for Blackstone as investment performance was solid, inflows were strong, and Perpetual Capital grew significantly. Quite recently, the stock sold off on President Trump's plans to ban institutional investors from buying single-family homes but it's discussed later that this was more than likely an overreaction. With the forward P/E now near multiyear lows, I believe there is considerable opportunity in BX stock, and so I'm upgrading to a Buy rating.

Q4 Financial Performance Was Decent

Let's start off today with a broad view at the company's financial performance in their 2025 Q4. First off, note that fee related earnings saw some weakness as there was a 16% decline YoY to $1.5 billion. Back in Q3, there was a quite significant increase YoY and so results in Q4 seem to be a little bit soft. However, while you can see that fee related performance revenues were a drag on fee related earnings overall, their management and advisory fees actually saw decent growth at 11% to show that their core business has remained resilient.

As for distributable earnings, Blackstone reported $2.2 billion, up 3% YoY. This is an indication that their profitability as a whole remained at respectable levels. Distributable earnings did see 48% of growth in the previous quarter, however, and so there is some deceleration. At the end of the day, Blackstone posted DEPS of $1.75, up 4% YoY. Despite deceleration from 50% of growth in Q3, they were still able to beat estimates by $0.21 to show that things were considerably better than expected.
