Overall, in my view, Q4 was a decent quarter from Blackstone but it was definitely nothing special for their financial results. When comparing key metrics to their counterparts in Q3, you can see that the trajectory of their financial performance has levelled off somewhat and that may be a potential reason for some cautiousness.

Inflows Were Robust

While short-term financial performance isn't insignificant, a look at their capital metrics may be even more indicative of the firm's health. As you can see above, there were significant inflows in each of their four segments to show that investor appetite seems to be strong at this point. This also points to how much trust clients place in them.

Another thing to point out is despite the fact that Real Estate has been generally a shaky area, they still saw inflows for Opportunistic, Core+, and Debt Strategies. We'll talk about their dry powder in just a second but do note that in Q4, there was more realizations than capital deployed in three of their four segments and so while the firm is continuing to invest, they also seem to be taking some profits. In the year ago quarter, they had $42 billion deployed versus $26 billion realized and so the firm's near term investment strategy seems to have shifted perhaps as a reflection of where asset prices are right now.

Dry power, as a whole, increased from $168.6 billion in the prior year period and from $188.1 billion in the previous quarter to $198.3 billion in Q4. This is a sign that Blackstone is more than ready to capitalize on any major opportunities that come up. With the exception of Multi-Asset Investing, all their segments saw an increase in Dry Powder from Q3. Even Real Estate saw a mild QoQ increase in dry powder and so I view the firm as well positioned if asset prices become attractive in the near term.

Investments Are Doing Well

While short-term investment performance may not be the best indication of long term performance, I believe taking a look at the numbers on the right do offer some insights. As you can see, all of their asset categories saw appreciation or positive gross returns with the exception of Real Estate Opportunistic. Therefore, in general, Blackstone seems to be making good investments and are delivering for clients. Even for Real Estate Opportunistic, the 0.3% depreciation is an improvement from Q3's 0.6% depreciation and so I would say that Q4 was a good quarter overall for their investments even though they really are focused on the long term for the most part.

AUM Continues To Climb

At the end of the day, most investors gravitate towards the AUM figures because they really do a great job at summing up where the firm really is at overall. In Q4, Blackstone's AUM increased by 13% YoY, outpacing Q3's 12% growth. Furthermore, inflows were stronger than in the previous quarter and as I stated earlier, that is a sign that investors are confident in their abilities. For Fee-Earning AUM, there was growth of 11% YoY and that's also an acceleration from Q3's 10% increase. Inflows also increased QoQ.

Lastly, as mentioned in my previous articles, Perpetual Capital is a critical area to monitor since outsized gains are possible when the firm doesn't have to worry about redemptions. In Q4, there was an 18% increase which is another acceleration of growth. Perpetual capital now represents 48% of Fee-Earning AUM, up both QoQ and YoY. Since this area is probably the most lucrative for Blackstone, this is clearly a positive sign.

Trump's Home Buying Ban Is Noise

Earlier this month, it was reported that Trump will ban institutional investors from buying single-family homes. Their stock plunged by 5.6% as a result of this news but in my view, this is mostly just noise. While Blackstone's portfolio of homes definitely isn't insignificant by any means, it also may not be as significant as investors think.

The firm stated that their "ownership of U.S. single-family homes represents about 2% of [their] real estate AUM and 0.5% of the overall firm" and that they "have also been a net seller of homes over the last decade – with [their] holdings down more than one-fifth." In my view, with single-family homes being such a minuscule part of their business, I view the plunge in the stock as an overreaction and so that may have in fact created considerable opportunity for investors.

Valuation Seems Too Low

As stated in the introduction, some readers called me out for using the P/B ratio to evaluate an asset-light firm in my previous article and so for today's analysis, let's switch to an arguably better measure, the forward P/E ratio. As shown above, the multiple has seen quite steep contraction recently and at a reading of just 22, I would say that there is a good amount of opportunity in the stock right now.

In their Q4, while financials like FRE or DE weren't the best when compared to historic results, I would say other metrics show that the firm is about as robust as it gets. Blackstone has seen solid inflows, their dry powder shows they are ready to pounce on opportunities, and their Perpetual Capital pile is as strong as ever. Therefore, I would say that the firm is well positioned.

As discussed above, the market believed the Trump ban on institutional home buying was a major headwind but in my view, that was a massive overreaction that has helped to pressure the valuation. As a result, the well below historic average level in the forward P/E shows that the risk/reward setup is significantly positive for investors in my view.

Recap

In my previous article, the hold rating was a reflection of my cautiousness on the valuation. Now that I have moved away from relying on the P/B ratio to analyze Blackstone, the conclusion is that the stock is quite attractive currently. As stated above, Q4 was a demonstration of resilience and robustness and Trump's plans to ban companies, like Blackstone, from buying homes really isn't that much of a concern. The overreaction has contributed to creating an opportunity for long-term investors, and so I have decided to upgrade BX stock to a Buy rating.

data.title: Public Storage's Slow Phase Is A Long-Term Opportunity
data.description:
Public Storage (PSA) is going through a transition where near-term organic growth remains soft, but long-term fundamentals and platform advantages are still intact. While margins are still high, the balance sheet is still strong, and dividends are well covered, there are some concerns around this phase of expansion through capital allocation (rather than rent health).

Current valuations sit near the lower end of PSA’s historical range, reflecting muted growth expectations. That opens up an opportunity for long-term investors to gain from the eventual normalization of margins in new properties and a supply tightness at the end of this phase that could boost organic rent growth.

What Supports PSA

PSA's same-store NOI margins are strong and steady. At 78.5% reported in Q3 2025, gross margins indicate that storage remains a high-margin business and that is holding over the past year (margins were 78.4% in Q3 2024). That enables PSA to protect profitability and cushions it in weak demand periods, reducing risks of earnings falling sharply.

Payroll and marketing costs are actually falling. The same store expenses data shows onsite payroll expenses down 6.1% and marketing expenses are lower by 4.1%. This shows efficiencies in progress and operational levers that can be extended to even newly acquired properties in the long run. For now, the operating cost leverage becomes even more important because PSA is potentially going through a phase of lower initial margins in newly acquired properties. Other operating costs, notably higher property taxes, are offsetting most of these gains. Property taxes have increased by 4.5% YTD and are expected to be as high as 5.5% for the full year across the same store portfolio.

The third supportive factor for PSA is that realized rent per occupied square foot is still holding up well even in a soft period—up 0.6% YoY. Existing customers rate increases are still sticking. There may be weaker pricing power in the case of new tenants, but existing customers are still paying higher over time and cushioning overall rental income.

Earnings Impact Gradual

PSA's growth engine looks modest overall and is supported primarily by external expansion and leasing activity rather than organic same-store growth. The development pipeline of 3.9m square feet and a remaining spend of ~381m over the next 18-24 months appears controlled but meaningful. However, this is not booming growth we are talking about.

The non-same store NOI growth is up by ~$47m while same store NOI is down slightly, indicating inorganic growth. This is therefore a growth era banking on capital deployment and execution rather than any structural cycle-driven rent expansion fundamentals. PSA is deploying ~$1.3b in acquisitions and ~$650m in the development pipeline. That exposes the growth prospects to demand and execution risks—not reliable growth from already stabilized assets. That also implies the earnings impact will be gradual as execution risks and initial lower margins weigh.

At the same time, organic fundamentals are not going through an expansion phase either. Same-store occupancy is drifting lower. Average occupancy is down 0.5% YoY (end-period occupancy down 0.7% YoY). This is not a material worry at this point but shows demand is not really improving meaningfully. Importantly, with supply not in shortage, a pricing recovery is unlikely to happen soon.

Balance Sheet Reasonably Controlled

Since PSA is now in a capital deployment phase, the balance sheet becomes an important area to monitor too. Over the last two and a half years, real estate assets have increased by ~$5.2b (up 20-25%), as is expected given the uptick in acquisition and development activity. A large part of this growth is funded by the balance sheet leverage. During the same period, net debt has grown by ~$3.5b. However, the earnings uplift is gradual while the asset base is rising fast—gradual because of initially lower occupancy and higher operating costs. This implies PSA will go through a period of elevated debt to EBITDA ratio. The current debt to EBITDA ratio is ~2.9x, which is reasonable by REIT standards, although it is expectedly higher than the 2-2.5x range we have seen before.

Overall, PSA still has a strong balance sheet but has had to increase leverage (rather than more retained cash flow) to fund expansion. The retained earnings have dropped from negative ~$160m to negative ~$1.1b from March 2023 to now, clearly showing how retained earnings are weaker after accounting for dividends and depreciation. At the equity level too, common equity has not grown in line with the asset base. Liquidity levels have also fluctuated through this period with cash balances moving up and down between quarters—implying dynamic capital deployment over idle liquidity during this asset expansion phase. None of these changes in PSA's capital structure are at levels yet that raise caution but need to be monitored, especially until earnings growth from new acquisitions starts to show up.

Dividends and Coverage

With an annualized core FFO of ~$17 and an annual dividend outflow of $12 ($3 monthly), the payout ratio is ~70% and retains a healthy cushion for the over 4% yields in the near term. However, yield alone does not clinch the thesis. PSA's price fluctuations are material, and 20-30% moves within a few months are not rare. That is, within a year, the price move alone is capable of wiping out 5-8 years of yields. The yield is stable, but valuations are a bigger determinant of fresh entry timing for PSA. Yields are an add-on to that Buy thesis on valuations.

Valuations Support Entry

Barring the sector-wide repricing effect in 2022-23, the price-to-FFO ratio is near the lowest healthy point seen in the past decade. PSA demands a premium relative to the sector median because of its conservative balance sheet and high margins. The platform play, wherein PSA has a proven ability to buy under-managed assets and lift margins, also contributes to that premium.

A lot of those factors that generally support PSA's valuations are still intact. The balance sheet is becoming only marginally more leveraged but still remains sufficiently below most REIT peers' leverage levels. The recent drop in valuations relative to history is potentially a reflection of markets factoring in weak same-store pricing power and a regime of growth mostly enabled by capital deployment rather than rent acceleration tailwinds.

That valuation compression is an opportunity for long-term investors with patient capital and the ability to ride volatility (even top-up positions if prices correct without any sharp change in fundamentals). While margins from new properties will only have a gradual effect on earnings in the short term, overall FFO can compound at 4-5% in the long term, both due to maturing operations in new assets and same-store improvements once supply slows. Such a regime could also open up scope for rerating in addition to FFO expansion itself. That is, fresh buys today can expect 4-5% from dividends and 4-5% from FFO expansion annually and over the next 2-3 years. That is high single-digit returns without including the impact of the valuation rerating optionality.

Overall, PSA is a decent Buy at current levels. But I will watch a few confirmation signals over the next few quarters to see whether the turn I expect is in progress. Starting with Q4 results, investors should see a narrowing of declines in move-in rent trends to -5% levels (and eventually flat at some point, if not immediately). In Q4, even minor improvement from the 10% declines reported in Q3 should count. Occupancy trends should also stabilize, i.e., stop drifting down. It is not a material drift as of now, but further drift should be arrested to retain the turnaround narrative. Leverage stabilization and non-same-store margin lifts will also be in focus.

I do not expect organic growth to return over the next few quarters till supply tightens. Somewhere around the end of the year, same-store revenue growth turning positive will be another key metric I will expect to show up. This Buy call in PSA may require multiple entries over the year if valuations compress further and depending on how the next few quarterly reports show margin improvements. PSA may not offer immediate upside, but it looks like a steady compounder—paying you to wait while gradual growth and a better industry backdrop slowly do their work.
